### SOX compliance — audit trails
All financial data processing must maintain complete audit trails per Sarbanes-Oxley requirements.
Log every transaction: who initiated it, what changed, the before/after values, and the timestamp.
Audit logs must be immutable — no modification or deletion. Store in append-only systems.
Implement separation of duties: the person who writes code should not deploy it to production.
Retain financial audit logs for minimum 7 years. Automate retention policies.
### Data encryption — at rest and in transit
All financial data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 and in transit using TLS 1.3.
Database encryption: use transparent data encryption (TDE) or application-level encryption for sensitive fields.
Never store encryption keys alongside encrypted data. Use a key management service (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault).
Rotate encryption keys on a defined schedule (quarterly recommended).
API endpoints handling financial data must enforce HTTPS — reject HTTP requests.
### No hardcoded credentials or financial data
Never hardcode database credentials, API keys, tokens, or financial account numbers.
Use environment variables with a secrets manager. Rotate credentials on a defined schedule.
Never log financial account numbers, routing numbers, or transaction amounts in debug logs.
Mask sensitive financial data in UI: show only last 4 digits of account numbers.
PCI DSS applies if handling credit card data: never store full card numbers, CVV, or magnetic stripe data.
### Transaction idempotency
All financial transaction endpoints must be idempotent — retrying the same request must not create duplicate transactions.
Use idempotency keys: clients send a unique key with each request, server deduplicates.
Store idempotency keys with results for at least 24 hours.
Return the original response for duplicate requests — do not process again.
This prevents double-charging, double-transfers, and duplicate ledger entries.
### Decimal precision for monetary values
Never use floating-point types (float, double) for monetary values — they cause rounding errors.
Use integer cents/minor units (store $10.50 as 1050) or a dedicated decimal library.
All monetary calculations must use exact arithmetic. Document the precision and rounding rules.
Currency must always be explicit — never assume USD. Store currency code alongside amount.
Display formatting is a UI concern — store raw values, format on display.
### Use Secure form handling
Use Secure form handling as part of the standard tech stack.

Use Card number masking as part of the standard tech stack.
